text.title: AR Anchoring Strategies
text.summary: Anchoring type matrix for AR experiences — horizontal surface, vertical surface, image tracking, object recognition, and geo — with use cases, fallback chains, and tracking quality rules.

rule.statement: Choose an anchoring type based on the use case and always define a fallback chain for when primary tracking fails.
rule.rationale: AR tracking degrades with poor lighting, low-texture surfaces, or underpowered devices. Without a fallback the user sees a blank screen or a jittering object.

example.code: Anchoring Type | Best Use Case | Fallback Horizontal plane | Furniture try-on, floor navigation | vertical plane → manual placement Vertical plane | Wall posters, window overlays | image marker Image tracking | Product packaging, business cards | object recognition → GPS/compass Object recognition| Tools, vehicles, printed logos | image marker Geo (VPS/GPS) | Outdoor navigation, city guides | GPS + compass only

rule.statement: Give the user scanning feedback within 2 seconds of AR mode launch; fall back to the next anchor type if tracking is not established within 8 seconds.
rule.rationale: Waiting silently erodes trust. Users do not understand why nothing appears. Progressive feedback ("Move device slowly") sets correct expectations.

list.item: Minimum 200 lux for indoor horizontal-plane tracking; warn user if environment is too dark.
High-contrast, non-reflective images work best for image tracking; warn and offer fallback on reflective surfaces.
Show "Looking for surfaces..." indicator during scan phase; replace with "Good tracking" confirmation on anchor.
Let user re-scan if tracking quality drops — expose a "Reset AR" control.

rule.statement: Snap AR content to detected surfaces; do not leave objects floating mid-air, as unstable placement causes motion discomfort and looks broken.
rule.rationale: Floating objects drift as tracking updates and appear unanchored, destroying the "AR feel" that makes spatial interfaces useful.

list.item: Horizontal surfaces (tables, floors): documents, 3D models, navigation paths.
Vertical surfaces (walls, doors): posters, dashboards, labels.
Respect physical boundaries — content should not clip through detected geometry.
Match content scale to detected surface size; provide user resize with pinch gesture.

section.title: WebAR Constraints

antipattern.description: Applying ARKit-spec patterns directly to WebAR without checking browser support.
antipattern.reason: iOS Safari does not support WebXR Device API; AR on iPhone must use AR Quick Look (model-viewer) or a native wrapper. WebAR has the worst tracking parity across platforms.

section.p: For WebAR: use <model-viewer> for iOS Quick Look + Android Scene Viewer as the baseline; upgrade to 8th Wall or Niantic Lightship for richer tracking where commercial license is available.
